label: ai
topic: Review of a psychological horror film with Hugh Grant as a sinister intellectual
text:
Hugh Grant has spent the last decade dismantling his own persona, trading the stammering romantic lead for increasingly uncomfortable characters — smarmy, manipulative, dangerous in ways that use his charm as camouflage. In "The Lesson," he completes this transformation so thoroughly that you may never watch "Four Weddings and a Funeral" the same way again.

Grant plays Richard Blackwell, a celebrated novelist and Oxford don who invites a young aspiring writer, Liam (Daryl McCormack), to spend the summer at his country estate as a private tutee. The arrangement is ostensibly literary. But from the first scene — Grant framed in the doorway of his study, backlit, smiling with the precise warmth of someone who has practised warmth — it is clear the lesson Blackwell intends has nothing to do with writing.

The first half is a masterclass in slow-building discomfort. Director Alice Troughton and cinematographer Adam Scarth shoot the estate as a place of oppressive beauty: long corridors, dark panelling, windows that let in light but seem to prevent escape. Grant presides over dinners and study sessions with the magnetic authority of a man accustomed to being the most intelligent person in any room, and who has learned to weaponise this.

Julie Delpy is quietly devastating as Blackwell's wife Hélène, a woman who has navigated her husband's manipulations for decades and developed a survival strategy that looks like complicity. The two young Mormon missionaries who appear periodically at the estate's door — earnest, scrubbed, radiating an innocence so complete it seems aggressive — function as a moral counterpoint. Their faith is literal and uncomplicated; Blackwell's is aesthetic and predatory. Troughton lets them linger just long enough for their presence to become unsettling, their politeness a mirror reflecting the estate's darker rituals.

McCormack holds his own against Grant, no small achievement. He plays Liam as someone intelligent enough to sense the trap and too ambitious to avoid it. The film is honest about how complicity operates in power imbalances — not as simple victimhood but as a slow series of concessions, each seemingly small, each narrowing the available exits.

The trouble arrives in the third act, when careful ambiguity gives way to convention. A revelation about Blackwell's past provides the explicit motivation the first half wisely left implicit, and the climax resolves into a confrontation that, while well-acted, feels like a screenplay fulfilling contractual obligations. The psychological horror of the first seventy minutes — the horror of being in the presence of someone smarter who has decided to use this advantage — gives way to plot mechanics.

This is frustrating because the strengths are considerable. Grant gives what may be the best performance of his career. He locates something genuinely frightening in Blackwell — not menace exactly, but the absence of boundaries between intellect and appetite. It is a fully committed performance in a film that, in its final act, loses its own commitment.

Scarth's cinematography deserves particular mention. He shoots interiors with a darkness that feels inhabited. A dinner scene lit almost entirely by candles achieves a chiaroscuro effect that would make Caravaggio nod. The estate itself becomes a character — beautiful, suffocating, a gilded cage that looks like privilege until you try to leave.

"The Lesson" is a film that is better than its ending. The first two-thirds are among the most accomplished psychological horror I have seen this year. The final third retreats to safer ground. It is a very good film that could have been a great one, which is perhaps its own kind of lesson.

Rating: 3.5 out of 5 stars.
